    Using Skills For Steering Solution

    I'm going to start by dictating ideas into the agent:

    I would like to take the instructions in CLAUDE.md and create a skill called

    coding-standards, which takes all of those disparate instructions and groups

    them together into different buckets. For instance, we have some that are more

    useful for the backend, some that are more useful for the frontend, and I want

    to create reference documents outside of the skill that the skill then

    references. This skill should be used whenever we reference coding standards, whenever

    we need to conduct a review, and whenever we do implementation, so this should

    be front and center of the LLM's mind. Once we've taken all of the stuff out

    of CLAUDE.md, we should be able to just delete CLAUDE.md or maybe leave a

    context pointer from CLAUDE.md to this skill.

    Building the Skill Structure

    Let's run this and see what happens.

    The agent went ahead and grouped the rules into five reference files and created a concise SKILL.md that points to them. Let's see what it generated.

    So inside coding-standards, we now have:

    • database.md - All the database coding standards
    • frontend-and-ui.md - Frontend and UI patterns
    • routes-and-forms.md - Routes and forms handling
    • services-and-testing.md - Services and testing standards
    • typescript.md - TypeScript conventions

    This is much more organized. Now the agent can pull what it needs when it needs it.

    Simplifying CLAUDE.md

    If we look at CLAUDE.md now, we just have a tiny little pointer focusing on the /coding-standards skill:

    Load that skill before writing code, reviewing changes, or answering questions about conventions.

    The skill description itself is quite large, so let's tighten it up. We can simplify it from:

    Project coding standards for this React Router v7 + Drizzle + SQLite codebase...

    To just:

    Project coding standards for this codebase...

    We don't need to list everything the description covers. And we can remove some redundant lines like "read the relevant reference file before producing code" since that's implied by the skill's purpose.

    Now the skill is nice and short. The description will be loaded directly into the context window, which is handy. And the skill is really just a set of reference files that link out to the coding standards.

    Context Architecture

    The setup we're creating here is kind of like a file system where the agent can pull more information as it needs.

    The agent sees the skill description straight away. That skill description can then link to the SKILL.md file, which then can link to the reference files. I call these little links "context pointers" because they're pointing to something interesting that the agent might need for its context.

    Context Pointer In Claude Code

    By adding an extra little pointer in CLAUDE.md, we kind of get to do double duty. This little thing in CLAUDE.md will be linking to the skill as well as the skill description.

    This makes it more likely that the skill is going to be picked up because it's got two context pointers pointing to it.

    Because we've got all this stuff saved in reference files, if those reference files grow, it doesn't bloat the parent's context. The agent only uses the stuff it needs when it needs it.

    Using This Skill

    I would recommend using this skill whenever you do any manual reviews. You can just say:

    Review this code, use the /coding-standards skill.

    Benefits of This Approach

    This is a great way to build up documentation about what you want your codebase to look like without drowning your CLAUDE.md file.

    This is even something you could share across an organization to make sure that everyone is doing the same coding standards and all the repos mostly look the same.

    The structure is:

    • Organized by domain - Each reference file covers a specific area
    • Lightweight - The main skill description stays concise
    • Reusable - Share across teams and projects
    • Scalable - Reference files grow without bloating the parent context
